text: It's no secret that the list of movies
I'm genuinely excited for in 2026 is
thinner than your average Hollywood
actress since the discovery of Vasmpic.
But one of the standouts for me was
Project Hail Mary, a science fiction
adventure based on an excellent novel of
the same name. It had, in theory at
least, all the elements I've been
craving from Hollywood in recent years.
a genuinely interesting concept based on
an original IP that isn't a sequel or a
reboot of some dinosaur franchise from
40 years ago. A complex narrative that
delves into a bit of hard science that
doesn't insult the audience's
intelligence. And all built around a
heartwarming story of friendship and
loyalty that transcends language,
culture, and even species in this case.
The only question was whether
co-directors Phil Lord and Chris Miller
would be able to do it justice. And the
answer, thank God, is yes. Project Hail
Mary is one of those rare beasts these
days that manages to strike the perfect
balance in pretty much every respect. A
big budget blockbuster that's more than
just a bunch of dumb characters in big
green rooms saying lines designed for
trailers, allowing the story to unfold
at its own pace without rushing or
getting bogged down. Delivering some
decent action set pieces without using
them as a crutch. working in a bit of
levity without devolving into parody,
emphasizing the importance of logic,
rationality, and science to overcome
problems, and most importantly, never
losing sight of the very human story at
its core. In short, it's a really godamn
good movie that actually made me feel
positive and optimistic by the time the
credits rolled. And to explain why,
allow me to furnish you with a
non-spoilery plot synopsis. The movie
opens cold with our main man, Dr. Ryland
and Grace waken up from an induced coma
aboard the spaceship the Hail Mary which
is now light years away from Earth
somehow. The rest of the crew died when
their life support machines failed on
route, leaving him alone with no memory
of how he got there, who he is, or what
he's supposed to be doing. But as his
memories slowly start to return, we
learn through a series of flashbacks
that planet Earth is in crisis. The
sun's energy is literally being consumed
by a space parasite known as astrophase.
And if something isn't done about it
soon, the planet will freeze within a
couple of generations and humanity will
be wiped out. The only hope is one
particular star about 11 light years
from Earth, which seems to be the only
one that isn't affected by the
astroofage. So, the Hail Mary was
created to travel there using the
astrophase itself as fuel, figure out
what the is happening, and
hopefully return the data to Earth so
they can replicate it in time to save
the worlds.
Boy, that escalated quickly, huh? No
pressure then. Grace has just come to
terms with all of this stuff when
suddenly he discovers he's not alone in
this solar system. Another spacecraft
from another world has also arrived
here. And before long they make the
first clumsy attempt to communicate with
him. What follows is a slow, often
humorous but always fascinating
relationship that develops between Grace
and Rocky, his fellow traveler, who he
eventually discovers was sent here on
the same mission as himself because his
own son is being consumed by the
astrophage as well. And given that
they're literally the only sentient
beings within like a billion miles, the
two agree to work together to find a
solution. But will they uncover the
secret to saving both their worlds in
time? And when unforeseen disasters
threaten them both, will Grace choose
loyalty to his new friend or his own
future? There's a lot of things to
praise about Project Hail Mary. And I
might as well start with the biggest and
most obvious one, the friendship between
Grace and Rocky, which forms the
emotional heart of the story and might
be one of the best examples I can think
of for how to create an empathetic but
genuinely alien character that you can't
help but like because he's actually well
written and well-developed instead of
looking like a person merged with a cat
and painted blue because it's cute. He's
basically a giant crab made out of
stone. He doesn't even have a
face, but he works as a character
because of a potent combination of
excellent writing and great puppeteering
that invests him with way more
personality and likability than the
entire Na'vi race combines. And I say
puppeteering because that's exactly what
they used here. He's actually a physical
presence in the movie instead of some
shitty CGI creation. He's funny, quirky,
and weird because he obviously doesn't
understand human culture. He's highly
intelligent, but in a way that functions
very differently from us. But he's also
loyal and trusting, willing to risk his
life for grace. And in a pretty
heartwarming little scene, he's
crushingly lonely and desperate for any
kind of interaction after years spent
alone, even if it's with a completely
alien species. It's this universal and
very human need for connection that
really drives the bond between these two
characters. And it lets you overcome the
fact that it's basically Ryan Goslin
interacting with a walking puppet.
Speaking of which, in a movie that's
pretty sparse on human characters,
Gossling obviously has to do most of the
heavy lifting here. And man, he
rises to the occasion pretty well. We're
more used to seeing him playing stoic,
quiet, brooden sort of characters. So,
it's a nice change of pace to watch him
pull off a more verbose, high energy
kind of role. He's highly intelligent
and resourceful, but he's also geeky,
scared, and overwhelmed by the task
he's facing. And well, you can kind of
understand why. He's definitely not a
hero when you first meet him. and he has
some very human moments of vulnerability
and self-doubt, but he grows into
something much stronger and better by
the ends. So, basically, you've got two
very contrasting but likable characters
with different skill sets who learn to
pull their resources to overcome a
seemingly impossible problem. I mean,
it's not exactly rocket science. Oh,
wait. I guess it kind of is, but it's
very effective. And when it's
implemented properly, it's a joy to
watch. You're instantly rooting for both
of these characters because they very
much earned it. There's a strong theme
running through Andy Weir's books about
the importance of rationality, science,
clear methodical thinking, and patient
determination to overcome massive
challenges. Just like in The Martian,
where Mark Wattney has to science the
out of his situation, patiently
solving one problem after another to
keep himself alive. So, Hail Mary
follows the same pattern. Grace and
Rocky not only have to overcome the
problem of mutual communication, but
also the much bigger challenges of
figuring out how to stop the astroofage
and how to get that information back to
their home planets. Not to mention a
bunch of unforeseen challenges and
disasters that hit them along the way.
It's a battle that's fought with hard
work creativity experimentation and
improvisation rather than some big
contrived action scene. Yeah, the film
does gloss over a few segments and plot
points from the book that would have
been too long-winded and tedious to show
on screen, but for the most part, it's
pretty faithful to the source
material. And I don't know, man. It's
just nice to see a big budget movie that
actually credits its audience with a bit
of intelligence and maturity for a
change. We used to get films like this
all the time, believe it or not, and I
think they're long overdue a resurgence.
Will you get more out of the experience
if you've read the book first? Probably,
but I don't think it's essential.
There's more than enough here for you to
grapple with as an audience all by
itself. Ultimately, I went into Project
Hail Mary with high hopes, and for once,
the film absolutely lived up to them.
It's an exciting, tense, funny,
heartwarming, and inspiring story of
friendship, sacrifice, and loyalty set
against the backdrop of an epic science
fiction adventure with the highest
stakes imaginable. A movie that tells
its story and tells it well. And for
that reason, it gets a drinker
recommends from me.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
